questions.question: Explain the concept of weak entity with an example and state how it is represented in ER diagram.
keyPoints: Weak entity depends on strong entity for identification
Example: Dependent entity depends on Employee
Has partial key combined with owner key
Represented by double rectangle

questions.question: Differentiate between composite attribute and multivalued attribute with examples.
keyPoints: Composite can be divided into simpler attributes (e.g., address→street,city)
Multivalued can have multiple values (e.g., phone_numbers)
Composite shown by oval connected to component ovals
Multivalued shown by double oval

questions.question: What is data independence? Explain its types with significance.
keyPoints: Ability to change schema at one level without affecting higher level
Physical data independence: changes in physical storage don't affect logical schema
Logical data independence: changes in logical schema don't affect application programs
Higher independence = more flexibility in system modification

questions.question: Explain cardinality and participation constraints in ER modeling with examples.
keyPoints: Cardinality: number of entities in relationship (1:1, 1:N, M:N)
Participation: whether entity must participate (total) or can participate (partial)
Example: Employee works in Department (1:N), Student enrolls in Course (M:N)
Total participation shown by double line, partial by single line

questions.question: Define primary key, candidate key, and foreign key with examples.
keyPoints: Primary key: unique identifier in relation (e.g., emp_id in Employee table)
Candidate key: attribute that can be primary key (e.g., emp_id, email)
Foreign key: attribute referencing primary key of another relation (dept_id in Employee referencing Department)
All maintain entity integrity and referential integrity

questions.question: Describe the three-schema architecture in database systems.
keyPoints: External schema: user view/application level
Conceptual schema: logical structure of entire database
Internal schema: physical storage details
Three-schema separates user view from physical storage

questions.question: Explain derived attributes with an example. How are they represented in ER diagram?
keyPoints: Derived attribute: value can be computed from other attributes
Example: age derived from date_of_birth
Shown by dashed/dotted oval in ER diagram
Not stored in database, calculated when needed

questions.question: Draw an ER diagram for a Banking System with entities: Customer, Account, Branch. Consider that a customer can have multiple accounts, an account belongs to one branch, and a branch can have multiple accounts. Include appropriate attributes and relationships with cardinality and participation constraints.
keyPoints: Entities: Customer (cust_id, name, address), Account (acc_no, balance, type), Branch (branch_id, branch_name, location)
Relationships: Customer_Account (M:N - customer can have multiple accounts)
Account_Branch (N:1 - each account belongs to one branch, branch can have many accounts)
Add primary keys, appropriate cardinality notations

questions.question: Explain the different types of data models in DBMS. Compare hierarchical, network, relational, and object-oriented models with advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Hierarchical: tree-like structure, one-to-many, rigid, good for hierarchical data
Network: graph structure, many-to-many, flexible but complex
Relational: tables with rows/columns, uses SQL, most popular
Object-oriented: stores objects, supports OOP concepts, complex
Comparison based on flexibility, complexity, query capability

questions.question: Design an ER diagram for a College Database System with entities: Student, Course, Instructor, Department. Include appropriate attributes and relationships. A student can enroll in multiple courses, a course can be taught by one instructor, an instructor belongs to one department, and a department offers multiple courses. Show all cardinalities and participation constraints.
keyPoints: Entities: Student(s_id, name, dob), Course(c_id, credits, name), Instructor(i_id, name, salary), Department(d_id, dname)
Relationships: Student-Course (M:N enrollment), Course-Instructor (1:1 teaches), Instructor-Department (N:1 belongs), Department-Course (1:N offers)
Include primary keys and relevant attributes
Total/partial participation as per scenario

questions.question: Explain the enhanced ER (EER) model concepts. How does it extend the basic ER model? Discuss specialization, generalization, and aggregation with examples.
keyPoints: EER extends ER with additional concepts for complex applications
Generalization: bottom-up process combining common features of entities into higher-level entity
Specialization: top-down process creating lower-level entities from higher-level
Aggregation: treating relationship as higher-level entity
Uses inheritance, disjoint/overlapping constraints
